author: @JohnnyAtlas
text:
I believe we used this gearbox to load coil springs into a shotblast motor would stop before output engagement when loading window turns output shaft attached to 4 paddles @ 90deg
So accurate and robust Might of had input clutch but output was always perfect 90 deg.Never knew what it  was until now. Ty Robert

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: The same drive was on the odometer of my 1965 Buick Estate Wagon. Wow that was a lot of miles ago.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: Great video! I am curious does the Geneva Drive have a specific ratio for the size of the slotted gear to the size of the rotating gear ?

author: @lorenbush8876
text: That is neat but don't you think it would be cheaper easier and quicker to use a cheap $1 solar calulator and a reed switch out of a free treadmill out of the trash and a magnet , I made one but I have not yet built the coil winder part , thats the part where the magnet goes to advance the counter. Thanks for the video.

author: @TommyApel
text: I remember having such a counter on my first bike to count kilometres ridden where the peg was on the wheel driving the sprocket once per revolution.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: So basically you invented an eventual lock pick, lol. Just kidding mate.😅

author: @zylascope
text: 3D printed computer! Wow :) Thanks for sharing.

author: @donbangert
text: We used a Geneva Drive for weighing craft beer cans on a filling line. It would rotate the can onto a load cell, while holding back the rest of the cans. The processor would then decide whether to pass or fail the can. If it failed, an air ram would eject it off the line.

author: @OriginalMorningStar
text: Ive been thinking for a while to make a scorer for golf using this mechanism, just two wheels to hold two rounds of the Par 3. I knew it was used in old cassette players and had something to do with a Maltese Cross but I couldnt find it. Geneva, no wonder lol. Ive now got the wheels designed but the reset mechanism is making my head hurt lol. Thanks for the reminders, up til now its actually a lot simpler than I thought.

author: @AlienLivesMatter
text: Drive it in reverse direction with a sucker turbine and overdrive a blower turbine off the counter-rotating output.

author: @viniciusnoyoutube
text: Very cool.

author: @simonmusgrovewethey
text: This was used in vehicles as odometers i e. counting the miles traveled. In this adaption there was a simple mechanism to prevent the numbered wheels from being wound backward to reduce the indicated mileage of a vehicle and confound disreputable car dealers.😊

author: @kadmow
text: - does this design have a reset function - handy to avoid needing a reversed coil wound every second run...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: not yet it is still a work in progress mate

text: Hi Robert, my son and I have been watching your many videos and they inspire him to create every day. Some older videos link to a FWG website which no longer seems to work, we wanted to get the FWG conductive ink but cant find it anywhere, could you point me in the right direction please?

author: @robertpitt8418

author: @sailaway8244
text: A simple way to charge a gravity battery directly from a wind turbine with a 1 to 9999 ratio or more with extra cogs 🤔

author: @pw.70
text: Is there anything mechanical that you don't know?! Fantastic piece of history and you always explain it all so well. Thank-you.

author: @Dalorath
text:
Hello Robert, I had an idea for you to maybe test out?? You know your double bladed wind generator you created recently? And the Omnidirectional generators? What if you take your larger bladed generator, and replace the blades with mounting poles that each hold an omni-directional generator?
Basically, this would mean you have a central generator with several omni directional generators spread out around the central hub.
I just thought it would an interesting experiment 😀

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text: Thank you for explaining that Robert!  I've been studying World War II mechanical cryptographic machines, but understanding how the wheels turned on those machines remained an...(sorry) Enigma!  🙂

author: @Theminecraftian772
text: Awesome!  I love that you mentioned a coil winder counter with this, because I've been thinking of 3d printing one of those that's completely mechanical in nature.  I do really hope that you're working on a coil winding machine, it would be so cool to have just a simple machine to do something that you seem to be regularly doing for your motors and generators.

author: @Bob.Jenkins
text: As beautiful as this concept for counting coil windings is... a simple and cheap alternative (Under £8) is to use a Digital Hand Held Tally Counter. It's very easy to Solder a micro switch to the 'Add' button and with it, you've a versatile, reliable and compact Winding Counter.

author: @MyNameIsLukey
text: Ayyy it looks so good! 🎉

author: @mikaelfransson3658
text: I never seen an open scotch yoke before! Sir Kelvin get it for a pro Quantum-computer! Christoffer Polhem did his variant for his saw! Its mind-blowing! Thanks Rob. /Mikael

author: @ThatTweedle
text:
wow, that is SURPRISINGLY SIMPLE!! I've never given it any thought, but if I had tried to figure out how it worked I don't think in 10,000 years would I have come up with the Geneva drive! lol
We are all standing on the shoulders of the giants that came before us. :)

author: @ez1913
text: How does a reset mechanism work? If it were connected to the coil winder, starting at zero would save a lot of math or running to the nearest even number.

author: @peterkent2138
text: The Geneva mech was well used in manufacturing, for table indexing on filling machines for example. Sadly with the advent of stepper and servo motors, not so common now.

author: @aaronsj80
text: Is that what is used in car odometers?

author: @timothyneiswander3151
text: Well, that beats the pants off my old counting method. Counting my fingers and toes because to get to 21 I had to drop my pants. Kinda makes you wonder how women count that high.

author: @crazyguysadvice
text: interesting how the master wheel takes the smallest steps, but the full amount of them. The whole 9 yards as they say! I just saw the like button flip up to 146 I've never seen it move, the likes o the video, it rolled up like the Geneva drive. What a nice coincidence. I'm like no. 147. I guess they've updated the GUI with more push-API elements for live updates on static elements of Youtube's stylesheet in relation to actively changing information in its database... I mean I've never noticed that thing change before live actively in front of me now it's 153 likes!

text: Babbage would be proud.

author: @mikedmann7487
text: That's one huge copper coil wrapping counter!

author: @pieters286
text: We use Geneva-drives a lot to ensure optical filters get acurately repeatable positioned in astronomical scientific instrumentation.

author: @AB-C1
text:
Excellent, that's how they used to "clock" cars, stick a drill in reverse to rewind the odometer so I understand. 
Great demo! 👍

author: @EvrttGrn
text: Awesome video.  It's also the mechanism for the older car odometer and what we called the flip number clocks, basically the predecessor to the current digital clocks.

author: @grendel1960a
text: and reverse the end its driven from to make the numbers appear correctly, ie so its not counting the single numbers in the thousands column

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: actually, if you flip the numbers the other way up and turn it end for end, that will work

text: Very cool! But doesn't this count the numbers starting on the wrong digit? Can you run it from the opposite side to count the 1's first instead of the 1000's?

author: @mark.guitar
text: I took one of these apart when my reel to reel tape recorder died back in the early seventies. I was fascinated then and I still am (a bit!). Now I know what it is called. Cheers Robert.
